text_blocks.text: However, Avicenna posited the brain as the place
where reason interacts with sensation. Sensation prepares the
soul to receive rational concepts from the universal Agent
Intellect. The first knowledge of the flying person would be * am,"
affirming his or her essence. That essence could not be the body,
obviously, as the flying person has no sensation. Thus, the
knowledge that "I am" is the core of a human being: the soul
exists and is self-aware. Avicenna thus concluded that the idea of
the self is not logically dependent on any physical thing, and that
the soul should not be seen in relative terms, but as a primary
given, a substance. The body is unnecessary; in relation to it, the
soul is its perfection. In itself, the soul is an immaterial
